role_summary: President of the Forrest County, Mississippi chapter of the NAACP (1970-1974, 2001-present); co-founder of the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement; sensitivity-training worker for the 1964 Freedom Summer volunteers in Hattiesburg; witness in the Forrest County voter-registration suit United States v. Theron C. Lynn; Hattiesburg public-school science teacher; 2025 honorary Doctor of Humane Letters from the University of Southern Mississippi.
ai_reading: The embedding's single strongest pull on Magee's testimony is not toward another NAACP officer but toward Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92), a Claiborne County subsistence farmer, whose passages register the highest cosine count to Magee's (121) in this corpus. The two men sat for the same interviewer (Emilye Crosby) in the same December 2015 week, and the vector signature seems to lock onto the long rural-Mississippi childhood that opens both interviews: clearing new ground with an axe, hauling spring water uphill, raising hogs and corn, never going hungry because the family grew everything. A schoolteacher and chapter president lands closest by cosine to a farmer who never held office, on the strength of shared upbringing rather than shared movement role. The influence graph adds a second, sharper reading: of the two outbound who-discussed-whom edges Magee's interview generates, the heavier by far runs to Vernon Dahmer, Jr. (entry 121) at a weight of 9, against a weight of 1 to Eddie Holloway (entry 33) and no inbound edges at all. Magee names the Dahmer family more than any other corpus figure, yet his own account is careful that he knew the slain Forrest County NAACP leader Vernon Dahmer Sr. "primarily through his sons," two of whom were his Alcorn classmates, rather than as a movement intimate. On the concept axes his positions sit near the rural-Southern and sacred ends (Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle and Sacred vs Secular both read toward the first pole), consistent with a Black-church-rooted Hattiesburg organizer, while the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis reads toward Tactical Pragmatism, picking up the hands-on local-organizing register that runs through the interview, the sensitivity-training sessions he ran for Freedom Summer volunteers, the years of poll-tax payments to stay on the rolls, the branch administration, rather than long-arc movement theorizing.
biographical_paragraph: Magee's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier, so the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript should be confirmed against external archives before citation; his external biographical record, however, is documented across the University of Southern Mississippi and the Historic Eureka School local-history archive [src: 1, src: 2, src: 7]. He was born in 1932 in Columbia, the seat of Marion County, Mississippi, the oldest of ten children in a farming family [src: 2]. He earned a bachelor's degree in biology from Alcorn A&M College (now Alcorn State University) in 1954, served in the U.S. Army during the Korean War era, stationed in the United States and in Germany, and in 1964 earned a master's degree in education from West Virginia University [src: 2]. He has described pursuing that graduate degree out of state on the GI Bill because the University of Southern Mississippi did not then admit Black graduate students [src: 1]. He and his wife Carrie Magee were among the few Black registered voters in Hattiesburg in the 1960s, having paid the poll tax for ten consecutive years, which led to their being subpoenaed in the federal suit against the Forrest County circuit clerk for denying Black applicants registration, the case United States v. Theron C. Lynn [src: 2, src: 7]. He helped found the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement and, during the summer of 1964, ran sensitivity-training sessions for Freedom Summer volunteers arriving in Hattiesburg, work he recounts in detail in his interview [src: 1]. He served as president of the Forrest County branch of the NAACP from 1970 through 1974 and was re-elected in 2001, holding the office since [src: 2, src: 6, src: 7]. He taught science in the Hattiesburg public schools, including at Lillie Burney and Rowan, alongside fellow corpus interviewee Eddie Holloway (entry 33), who names Magee as his tenth-grade science teacher; he later worked for the Southern Mississippi Planning and Development District and the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Food and Consumer Service, retiring in 1998 [src: 2, src: 7]. The University of Southern Mississippi conferred an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters on him at its spring 2025 commencement [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: Magee describes one of his repeated attempts to register to vote at the Forrest County clerk's office in the late 1950s:
interview_snippets.quote: I told her, she called him from the back, he walked up, I'm standing there, he says, what do you want, boy?

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the orientation work he and other local people did for Freedom Summer volunteers coming into Hattiesburg in 1964:
interview_snippets.quote: Since we knew the culture, we did a lot of sensitivity training

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether he knew Vernon Dahmer Sr., the Forrest County NAACP leader murdered by the Klan in January 1966:
interview_snippets.quote: I knew Vernon Dahmer, but I knew him primarily through his sons.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling how several men took turns watching the Dahmer property in the nights after the firebombing:
interview_snippets.quote: shortly after they burned his house, and stood watch.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: On what he sees as the obligation that comes with the rights the movement won:
interview_snippets.quote: Freedom you think you have can be lost if you do nothing. Because freedom incurs a huge responsibility.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked what he considers the most significant issue still facing his community, the retired science teacher answered by repetition:
interview_snippets.quote: The first word is education. The second word is education. And by now you know the third word is education.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eddie Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fellow Hattiesburg interviewee Eddie Holloway, asked to describe Magee, places him first as a classroom teacher:
interview_snippets.quote: I remember Mr. McGee. He was my 10th grade science teacher.

movement_context: Magee became president of the Forrest County NAACP in 1970, four years after the chapter's earlier president, Vernon Dahmer Sr. (father of catalog entry 121's interviewee Vernon Dahmer Jr.), was murdered by the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan on January 10, 1966; Dahmer Sr. had served two terms as Forrest County branch president [src: 3]. The murder followed Dahmer Sr.'s January 9, 1966 radio announcement that he would pay the poll tax on behalf of Black residents who feared registering at the Forrest County courthouse; his home and store were firebombed before dawn on January 10 by Klansmen, and he died the next day of injuries sustained covering his family's escape [src: 3]. Samuel Bowers, the Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan of Mississippi, was identified as having ordered the killing [src: 3, src: 5]. Magee, in his own interview, recounts that he knew Dahmer Sr. mainly through two of Dahmer's sons who were his classmates at Alcorn, and that he was among the men who kept watch near the Dahmer property in the nights after the firebombing [src: 1]. The 1964 Freedom Summer sensitivity-training Magee describes was the Hattiesburg-local instance of the broader Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) volunteer-orientation effort; the statewide program also ran residential sessions at Western College for Women in Oxford, Ohio, the site where Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney had trained before the June 1964 Neshoba County murders [src: 1, src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: Samuel Bowers was convicted in a Mississippi state court in 1998 for ordering the 1966 Dahmer murder, after earlier prosecutions in the late 1960s had failed to produce a conviction on that charge; the reopened case was carried by the state and federal authorities decades after the Klan campaign Magee's chapter continued organizing against [src: 3, src: 5]. Magee's advocacy for affordable housing through the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement contributed to the establishment of the Francis Street Apartments in Hattiesburg, and he was a charter member and first secretary of the 1965 community-action committee that became the local anti-poverty agency [src: 2]. In his interview he describes his continuing work, into his eighties, on community-policing dialogue between Hattiesburg residents and law enforcement, framing it as an effort to keep the city from a confrontation like the one in Ferguson, Missouri [src: 1]. The University of Southern Mississippi conferred an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters on him at its spring 2025 commencement, citing his record in education and civil rights; he has continued to serve as Forrest County NAACP branch president since 2001 [src: 2, src: 7].

gallery.alt: Vernon Dahmer Sr. funeral, Shady Grove Baptist Church, Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 15, 1966
gallery.caption: The graveside funeral service for Vernon Dahmer Sr. at Shady Grove Baptist Church in Kelly Settlement near Hattiesburg, Mississippi on January 15, 1966, five days after his death from injuries sustained in the January 10 White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan firebombing of his home and general store. Dahmer Sr. had been president of the Forrest County NAACP branch; Magee became the branch's president in 1970, and in his interview recalls keeping watch near the Dahmer property in the nights after the firebombing. Photograph from the Mississippi Department of Archives and History civil rights collection.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

gallery.alt: Forrest County Courthouse and Confederate Monument, Hattiesburg, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Forrest County Courthouse and Confederate Monument in Hattiesburg, Mississippi (NRHP Hub City Historic District contributing property 7, reference 80002237). The 1966 Forrest County poll-tax office Vernon Dahmer Sr. had announced his Kelly Settlement store would replace was located in this courthouse; the broader school-desegregation negotiation work Magee participated in through the 1960s and 1970s ran through the same county-government infrastructure.
